title: Responsible AI & EU/Swiss Readiness

introduction.subtitle: Governance readiness assessment with jurisdiction branching. 7-9 min.
introduction.description:
Responsible AI & EU/Swiss Readiness helps you learn the vocabulary and typical expectations behind Classification & Scope, Data & Privacy, Risk & Human Oversight, Documentation & Monitoring. Use it as a private diagnostic: answer from observable evidence, not aspiration, and treat the result as a map for the next improvement cycle.

The score stays separate from the optional answer-support check, which asks whether your answers are based on opinion, anecdotes, documentation, or system-verified evidence. A high answer should mean the practice is visible, repeatable, and useful in real decisions.

This educational quiz runs entirely in your browser. You receive the baseline result without account creation or email.
introduction.duration: 8 diagnostic questions plus 4 evidence checks. Approximately 7-9 min.
introduction.privacyNote: Your answers stay in this browser. The result belongs to you. Sharing is explicit and share-safe.
introduction.disclaimer: Educational diagnostic only. Not legal, tax, financial, investment, medical, employment, regulatory, or psychometric certification advice.

questions.text: How complete is the inventory of AI systems, models, and material AI-enabled features?

options.text: A. The information is fragmented, missing, or too inconsistent to support decisions.
B. Partial tools or reports exist, but definitions and ownership are weak.
C. Definitions, owners, access rules, and regular review are governed.
D. Lineage, quality controls, exception handling, and a feedback loop into decisions exist.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Classification & Scope: How complete is the inventory of AI systems, models, and material AI-enabled features
conceptHelp.summary: Classification & Scope means knowing which AI systems exist, what they do, who is legally responsible, and which risk class or jurisdiction may apply. This question narrows the concept to "How complete is the inventory of AI systems, models, and material AI-enabled features?" so you can judge the real practice, not the ambition around it.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Classification & Scope matters because founders, investors, partners, and teams need to know whether this area can guide decisions under pressure, not just sound good in a plan.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Use recent observable evidence: decisions, customer conversations, metrics, owners, records, reviews, policies, deal materials, or shipped work. Choose a high answer only when the evidence has changed a real choice.
conceptHelp.example: A strong answer would show the current AI practice for "How complete is the inventory of AI systems, models, and material AI-enabled features?", with owners, controls, evidence, and a recent deployment, monitoring, or rejection decision.

questions.text: How consistently are legal role, jurisdiction, and risk classification assessed?

options.text: A. The area is mostly unmapped, reactive, or dependent on informal trust.
B. The area is known in broad terms, but controls, owners, or evidence are incomplete.
C. Owners, controls, thresholds, and response or remediation paths are documented.
D. Controls are tested, monitored, reviewed after incidents or changes, and integrated into operating governance.
Not sure / not applicable.

questions.text: How well are training, input, retrieval, and output data rights governed?

options.text: A. Authority is unclear, improvised, or concentrated in one person.
B. The practice is informal; boundaries, escalation, or records are inconsistent.
C. Owners, decision rules, and review points are documented for important cases.
D. Authority, evidence standards, exception handling, and periodic review are governed.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Data & Privacy: How well are training, input, retrieval, and output data rights governed
conceptHelp.summary: Data & Privacy means governing training data, inputs, retrieval, outputs, rights, privacy impacts, and data protection inside AI use. This question narrows the concept to "How well are training, input, retrieval, and output data rights governed?" so you can judge the real practice, not the ambition around it.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Data & Privacy matters because founders, investors, partners, and teams need to know whether this area can guide decisions under pressure, not just sound good in a plan.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Use recent observable evidence: decisions, customer conversations, metrics, owners, records, reviews, policies, deal materials, or shipped work. Choose a high answer only when the evidence has changed a real choice.
conceptHelp.example: A strong answer would show the current AI practice for "How well are training, input, retrieval, and output data rights governed?", with owners, controls, evidence, and a recent deployment, monitoring, or rejection decision.

conceptHelp.summary: Risk & Human Oversight means identifying AI safety, bias, misuse, robustness, and human-control risks in a way that changes deployment decisions. This question narrows the concept to "How systematically are safety, bias, robustness, and misuse risks evaluated?" so you can judge the real practice, not the ambition around it.

options.text: A. This is not explicit yet in Risk & Human Oversight; decisions depend on intuition, habit, or one-off judgement.
B. There is a plausible practice in Risk & Human Oversight, but evidence, ownership, or repeatability is still partial.
C. Risk & Human Oversight has a documented practice with examples, owners, and current evidence used in decisions.
D. Risk & Human Oversight is reviewed, measured over time, and strong enough to guide trade-offs under pressure.
Not sure / not applicable.

contextQuestions.text: Which jurisdiction context should frame your result?

options.text: EU/EEA
Switzerland
UK
Other or uncertain

conceptHelp.title: Jurisdiction context
conceptHelp.summary: Jurisdiction context means the legal and regulatory setting used to interpret the responsible-AI questions. EU/EEA, Switzerland, UK, and other contexts can imply different duties, terms, and risk expectations.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: The same AI practice may be interpreted differently depending on the rule set. Choosing the closest context makes the result easier to read as a learning map.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Choose the jurisdiction that most strongly shapes your organisation, product, customers, or deployment plans. If you are unsure, choose the uncertain option rather than guessing too narrowly.
conceptHelp.example: A Swiss startup selling an AI product into EU customers may need to think about both Switzerland and EU/EEA expectations; this context answer helps frame that ambiguity.

contextQuestions.text: Which AI role is closest to your organisation?

options.text: Provider
Deployer
Importer/distributor
Uncertain

conceptHelp.title: AI role in the value chain
conceptHelp.summary: AI role means whether the organisation builds the AI system, deploys it, imports or distributes it, or is still unsure. Different roles usually carry different practical responsibilities.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: A founder or team can misunderstand readiness if they answer as the wrong actor. Builders, deployers, and distributors often need different evidence, controls, and documentation.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Choose the role closest to what your organisation actually does with the AI system today. If more than one role applies, choose the one with the most responsibility or uncertainty.
conceptHelp.example: A company using a third-party AI tool internally is closer to a deployer; a company training and selling its own model is closer to a provider.

contextQuestions.text: What level of evidence supports your answers for Classification & Scope?

options.text: Opinion: mostly judgement, memory, or individual perception.
Anecdotal: examples exist, but they are not systematically documented.
Documented: there are current notes, models, policies, metrics, or records.
System-verified: evidence is routinely produced by a governed workflow, system, or review cadence.

conceptHelp.title: Classification & Scope
conceptHelp.summary: This question asks how well your answers about Classification & Scope are supported. Classification & Scope means knowing which AI systems exist, what they do, who is legally responsible, and which risk class or jurisdiction may apply. The goal is learning: separate what you know from what you suspect, remember, or still need to verify.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: A result about Classification & Scope is more useful when you know whether it rests on opinion, a few examples, current documents, or system-verified evidence. Weak support does not make the answer useless; it tells you what to learn next.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Choose the strongest support you actually have right now. Do not upgrade an answer because the practice feels important, and do not downgrade it if current records or systems really exist.
conceptHelp.example: If your Classification & Scope answer is based on a recent metric, policy, deal document, customer record, or operating review, choose documented or system-verified. If it is mostly memory or judgement, choose opinion or anecdotal.

questionStageTitle: Learn the concept, then answer from evidence
questionStageDescription: Use the concept button when a term is unfamiliar. Prefer observable evidence over aspiration or ideal self-description.
contextDescription: This step is not scored. It records what your diagnostic answers are based on: opinion, anecdote, documentation, or system-verified evidence. Use it to decide how much to verify the result before acting on it.
